manifest.name: XanaNode Canonical Substrate
manifest.description: The canonical XanaNode substrate for protocol vocabulary, project identity, governance, software stack records, source snapshots, branding, and self-referential examples.

nodes.title: Non-Destructive Merge Review
nodes.summary: A review state where overlapping nodes are mounted together, compared, and kept distinct until a human approves stronger identity claims.

nodes.summary: A short trail showing that mounted overlap can stay visible and reviewable without forcing a final merge.

nodes.summary: The same information arranged through different relationships can produce different epistemic capabilities.

nodes.summary: A way of navigating knowledge by following meaningful associations rather than only linear order or hierarchy.

nodes.summary: A structure that determines how information relates, persists, and can be navigated.

nodes.title: Relationships Preserve Context

nodes.title: As We May Think
nodes.summary: Vannevar Bush's 1945 essay describing associative trails and the problem of navigating expanding scientific knowledge.
nodes.author: Vannevar Bush

nodes.title: Start Here
nodes.summary: A short curated route introducing the substrate through source, claim, supporting context, and concept.

nodes.title: Knowledge Substrate
nodes.summary: A structure for preserving relationships among knowledge objects.

nodes.title: Researcher A
nodes.summary: The local actor responsible for assertions in federation example substrate A.

nodes.title: Memory Substrate
nodes.summary: A system for preserving context, lineage, and navigable memory over time.

nodes.title: Researcher B
nodes.summary: The local actor responsible for assertions in federation example substrate B.

nodes.title: The canonical substrate assembles federated stack-layer substrates
nodes.summary: The canonical substrate is no longer only a monolithic description of XanaNode; it explicitly points to the real stack-layer substrate sources that make the system work.
nodes.content: The canonical substrate should teach that the stack is composed, not merely listed. Its project nodes describe the layers, and its federated substrate source nodes point at the real repositories and substrate roots for those layers. That lets people inspect the stack as a working federation of independently authored and versioned parts instead of assuming the canonical bundle is only a flat merge.

nodes.title: The canonical substrate distinguishes embedded examples from federated sources
nodes.summary: Embedded teaching namespaces inside the canonical bundle are different from the real stack-layer substrate sources represented as federation targets.
nodes.content: The canonical bundle contains several kinds of composition at once. Namespaces such as example.minimal, example.researcher_a, and example.researcher_b are embedded inside the bundle for teaching, testing, and portable inspection. By contrast, the Protocol, Core, Workspace, Studio, Mobile, and Hugo substrate entries point to real independently versioned substrate sources. That distinction matters because it tells readers what is bundled here and what exists as its own substrate outside the bundle.

nodes.title: Copy and paste breaks lineage
nodes.summary: When knowledge is reused by duplication instead of reference, provenance, attribution, surrounding context, and revision history become harder to preserve.
nodes.content:
Ordinary reuse usually means making another anonymous copy. Once a quote, summary, screenshot, or excerpt is pasted into a new document, the connection back to the exact source version often becomes social rather than structural.

XanaNode treats that break as a core failure mode. Addressable fragments, typed relationships, and transclusion let a later projection show not only the reused passage, but where it came from, which version it came from, and how it is being used now.

nodes.title: Document boundaries collapse knowledge structure
nodes.summary: When a document is treated as the main unit of knowledge, many of the relationships that give meaning to the material become implicit, hidden, or lost.
nodes.content:
A page, file, note, or database row can store information, but it does not automatically preserve why one idea supports another, where a quote came from, who disagreed, or what later changed. Those relationships usually live in a human reader's head or in conventions that a machine cannot reliably inspect.

That is why document-centric systems feel good for publication but weak for durable knowledge transfer. The information survives. The structure that made it useful often does not.

nodes.title: Fragments can address ranges, timecodes, and media regions
nodes.summary: XanaNode fragments can point to granular spans such as quoted text, word ranges, timecoded audio/video segments, and other media-native chunks, not only paragraph blocks.
nodes.content: The protocol already separates durable addressing from extraction detail. The tumbler identifies the exact fragment and source version. The selector describes how to locate the chunk inside that source version. That lets a fragment represent a quoted sentence, a word or character range, a page region, or a timecoded media segment while preserving one interoperable transclusion model.

nodes.title: Implementations Identify XanaNode Compatibility
nodes.summary: Implementations should identify themselves as XanaNode-compatible and reference the canonical specification without implying official ownership.

nodes.title: Local-first without federation is not enough
nodes.summary: Owning local files is important, but durable knowledge transfer also requires a shared protocol for portability, identity, raw assets, and federation.
nodes.content:
Local-first tools solve a real problem: people should own their data. But ownership alone does not create interoperability. If a tool stores files locally without a strong substrate model for node identity, relationships, fragments, assets, and exchange, the knowledge still tends to stay trapped inside that tool's habits and assumptions.

XanaNode tries to keep the benefits of local-first authorship while adding a protocol layer that allows federation, mounting, merging, projection, and transport without discarding meaning.

nodes.title: A possible match preserves local identity until review
nodes.summary: Overlap can be recorded as a possible match while both local concepts remain intact pending human review.
nodes.content: Knowledge Substrate and Memory Substrate are similar enough to map but not safe to collapse automatically. The merge report records a possible match, the compatibility report says the two substrates can be analyzed together, and the diff records the added nodes and relationships. None of those artifacts overwrites either author's local node. That is the intended merge posture.

nodes.title: Projections are lenses over a stable substrate
nodes.summary: Studio, Mobile, Hugo, and future tools are projections of the same substrate rather than separate competing sources of truth.
nodes.content:
A projection is the interface a person happens to use, not the canonical knowledge itself. The same substrate should be able to appear as a desktop workbench, a mobile capture tool, a static website, a printed booklet, or another compatible interface without rewriting the underlying node identities, relationships, fragments, or media.

This is why XanaNode separates the substrate from the projection layer. The lens can change. The knowledge should not have to start over.

nodes.title: Protocol Docs Are Licensed CC-BY 4.0
nodes.summary: XanaNode protocol documentation is licensed under CC-BY 4.0 so the docs can be shared and adapted with attribution.

nodes.title: Relationships Preserve Knowledge
nodes.summary: Knowledge survives movement between tools when relationships carry meaning, evidence, authorship, and addressable context instead of collapsing into anonymous links.
nodes.content:
Relationships preserve knowledge because they carry the structure that explains why one thing matters to another. A claim is not equivalent to its source, a person is not equivalent to the work they authored, and a fragment is not equivalent to the whole document it came from.

If a system preserves only isolated notes or files, much of that meaning dissolves during copying, migration, summarization, or machine processing. When the typed relationships remain intact, the knowledge can still be inspected as a coherent structure with evidence, disagreement, provenance, lineage, and context.

nodes.title: Schemas, Validators, And Reference Code Are Apache-2.0
nodes.summary: XanaNode schemas, validators, tooling, and reference implementation code are licensed under Apache-2.0 for broad adoption.

nodes.title: The XanaNode stack demonstrates federation through layer substrates
nodes.summary: The major XanaNode layers are published as their own substrate sources, so the stack records federation in live substrate targets rather than only describing it in prose.
nodes.content: Protocol, Core, Workspace, Studio, Mobile, and Hugo each publish or point to their own substrate source. The canonical substrate can therefore link to real substrate targets for the stack, not only to prose about the stack. This is a stronger proof than a diagram alone because each layer is addressable, versionable, and independently federatable.

nodes.title: XanaNode is an open kernel for knowledge
nodes.summary: XanaNode should function as a shared substrate layer for knowledge the way Linux functions as a shared kernel layer for computing: open, reusable, and separable from any one end-user interface.
nodes.content:
The Linux comparison matters because it shifts XanaNode out of the category of one more application. The protocol, Core SDK, workspace behavior, fragment model, and federation rules are intended to become the shared plumbing underneath many projections rather than a single locked experience.

That is why the substrate must remain durable when a viewer changes. If the underlying knowledge survives the loss of one projection, the protocol is doing the job a kernel-like substrate layer is supposed to do.

nodes.title: XanaNode Name And Logo Are Trademarked
nodes.summary: The XanaNode name, logo, and related brand assets are governed as trademarks even when protocol docs and code are openly licensed.

nodes.title: XanaNode Contributors
nodes.summary: The people and future maintainers who author, review, preserve, and project XanaNode-compatible substrates.

nodes.title: Addressable Fragment Selectors
nodes.summary: Selectors carry the precise in-source chunking detail for a fragment, while the tumbler preserves the durable address of the exact source version and fragment version.
nodes.content:
A XanaNode fragment is not limited to a paragraph block. The same tumbler model can point to a quoted sentence, a character range, a word range, a page region, an image region, or an audio/video time span.

The durable identity comes from the versioned fragment tumbler. The selector explains how the implementation found or preserved that exact span inside the source version. That is how XanaNode gets closer to Nelson's finer-grained reuse model without inventing a separate address family for each medium.

nodes.title: Attribution License
nodes.summary: A license pattern that allows reuse while requiring credit to the original author or project.

nodes.title: Compatibility Attribution
nodes.summary: The convention that implementations should identify themselves as XanaNode-compatible and point to the canonical specification without implying ownership or endorsement.

nodes.title: Content License
nodes.summary: A license applied to authored text, documentation, examples, or explanatory material rather than executable reference code.

nodes.title: Embedded Example Namespaces
nodes.summary: The canonical substrate carries small embedded teaching namespaces directly inside its own bundle without pretending they are live mounted external substrates.
nodes.content: The canonical substrate includes preserved namespaced records from example.minimal, example.researcher_a, and example.researcher_b. They are embedded so readers and tools can inspect small substrate structure and federation patterns in one portable bundle. That is different from the real stack-layer substrate sources, which are represented as independently versioned federation targets.

nodes.title: Knowledge Substrate
nodes.summary: The graph-shaped structure that lets information remain understandable across time by making claims, sources, media, fragments, and relationships explicit.
nodes.content:
A knowledge substrate is not the same thing as a pile of information. It is the structure that makes information navigable, reviewable, and preservable over time.

In XanaNode, a substrate keeps nodes, relationships, raw assets, fragments, provenance, and authorship together so meaning does not disappear when the material moves between tools or formats. The same facts arranged through different relationships can produce very different explanatory power, which is why the structure itself matters.

A substrate can be authored locally, transmitted as a portable bundle, mounted from another source, or projected into a site, app, archive, or physical medium. The substrate is the underlying knowledge; projections are only lenses over it.

nodes.title: Licensing
nodes.summary: The legal layer that describes how documents, schemas, code, media, and names may be copied, reused, attributed, modified, or governed.

nodes.title: Non-Destructive Merge Review
nodes.summary: A federation pass should surface overlap, compatibility, and candidate mappings without silently collapsing distinct local nodes.
nodes.content: XanaNode merge behavior is review-first. A federation pass can detect overlap, record possible matches, score compatibility, and show a structural diff without pretending two authors meant exactly the same thing. That is why merge reports, compatibility reports, and diffs are protocol artifacts in their own right: they let tools expose the overlap before any permanent collapse or absorption happens.

nodes.title: Open License
nodes.summary: A license that grants broad public permission to use, share, adapt, or implement a work while preserving stated conditions such as attribution or notices.

nodes.title: Permissive Open Source License
nodes.summary: An open source license family that allows broad reuse, modification, distribution, and commercial adoption with limited obligations.

nodes.title: Protocol Artifacts
nodes.summary: The portable JSON files, schemas, fragments, reports, and media references that allow tools to validate, compare, mount, import, merge, and render substrates.
nodes.content:
Protocol artifacts are the portable files that make a XanaNode substrate inspectable by both people and machines. They include the manifest, node records, relationship lists, fragments, validation reports, suggestions, registries, media references, and any other standardized outputs the protocol defines.

These artifacts matter because tools should not need private in-memory state or one specific application in order to understand a substrate. If the artifacts are present and valid, another compatible tool should be able to open the substrate, validate it, compare it, federate it, or project it.

When the protocol stack is working properly, the artifacts are not a side effect. They are part of the knowledge being preserved and transmitted.

nodes.title: Self-Federating Stack
nodes.summary: The XanaNode stack is federated because its major layers are represented as independently versioned substrates that can still interoperate as one system.
nodes.content: The stack itself is federated. Protocol, Core, Workspace, Studio, Mobile, Hugo, Canonical, Alphabet, and Lineage are all represented as distinct substrates or substrate targets with their own namespace, repository, transport shape, and governance path. The canonical bundle also carries embedded teaching namespaces for inspection and testing, but those are not the same thing as the real stack-layer substrate sources. Together, these patterns show that each layer can stand on its own while the layers can still be mounted, compared, validated, projected, and carried together without collapsing into one monolithic blob.

nodes.title: Software License
nodes.summary: A license applied to source code, validators, tooling, schemas, and implementation artifacts.

nodes.title: Substrate Projection Layer
nodes.summary: A renderer or lens that presents a substrate for a particular use without becoming the substrate itself.

nodes.title: Trademark
nodes.summary: A protected name, logo, or mark that identifies the source and reputation of a project, product, organization, or community.

nodes.title: The Trifecta of Computing Failure
nodes.summary: A three-part failure pattern in modern computing where knowledge is trapped in isolated documents, copied without durable lineage, and kept in tools that do not naturally federate.
nodes.content:
The Trifecta of Computing Failure names the pattern XanaNode is trying to escape.

First, most systems treat the document or file as the primary container of meaning. Second, when people need to reuse or synthesize knowledge, they usually copy and paste excerpts into new places, which severs provenance, surrounding context, and revision lineage. Third, even tools that improve local note-linking rarely provide a durable protocol for independent authorship, portable raw assets, and federation across many tools and owners.

XanaNode is meant to answer all three at once: relationships become first-class, transclusion and fragment identity preserve lineage, and the substrate can move between projection layers without collapsing back into one app's private format.

nodes.title: XanaNode
nodes.summary: A protocol for independently authored knowledge substrates that preserve relationships, provenance, lineage, disagreement, and addressable fragments so knowledge can move across tools and media without losing its structure.
nodes.content:
XanaNode is a protocol for independently authored knowledge substrates that preserve relationships, provenance, lineage, disagreement, and addressable fragments, so knowledge can move across tools and media without losing its structure.

The protocol does not define a single viewer or one mandatory application. It defines the durable substrate shape, the validation rules, the registries, and the artifact contracts that let many tools read, compare, merge, and project the same knowledge.

A XanaNode-compatible stack should be able to carry people, places, things, ideas, claims, questions, evidence, uncertainty, raw assets, and typed relationships in a form that survives transmission. That is why the protocol centers relationships, authorship, source lineage, and stable references instead of treating a document as the only meaningful unit.

nodes.title: What Is XanaNode?
nodes.summary: An explanation of XanaNode as a layered protocol, not a single viewer, website, repository, or graph visualization.
nodes.content:
XanaNode is a layered protocol and working stack for knowledge substrates. It is not the website, not the graph alone, not the Hugo projection, and not a single repository.

At the bottom, the protocol defines the shared rules: canonical schemas, registries, artifact contracts, validation expectations, and governance conventions. Core reads and writes those protocol records. Workspace manages substrate folders, bundles, and federation intake. Studio and Mobile are authoring tools built on top of that substrate model. Hugo is one projection layer that renders the same underlying substrate as a public static site.

That layering matters because the knowledge should remain usable even when one projection changes. A substrate can be moved to another tool, shared as a `.substrate` bundle, mounted from a registry target, or rendered through a different projection without rewriting its meaning from scratch.

XanaNode is also an explicit answer to a three-part computing failure: document boundaries collapse knowledge structure, copied excerpts lose durable lineage, and local-first tools without a shared substrate protocol still do not federate cleanly. The protocol tries to solve that by keeping relationships first-class, making fragments addressable, preserving raw assets and provenance, and separating the substrate from any one projection layer.

The canonical XanaNode substrate is intentionally self-referential. It carries its own schemas, artifacts, sources, branding, project nodes, implementation links, and explanatory trails so the stack can demonstrate the thing it claims to provide.

nodes.title: XanaNode As A Kernel For Human Thought
nodes.summary: A canonical essay explaining XanaNode as an open substrate layer for knowledge, with stable fragment transclusions that tie the comparison back to the authored source text and the protocol's core claims.
nodes.content:
XanaNode makes more sense when compared to Linux than when compared to another notes app.

Linux became important because it was not merely a product. It was an open kernel: a shared plumbing layer other people could build on, distribute differently, and keep using even when one interface, company, or distribution changed. Android, servers, embedded devices, and supercomputers all diverged at the projection layer while still relying on the same foundational contract underneath.

XanaNode is trying to do that for knowledge.

The protocol defines the common contract. Core enforces the substrate shape, fragment model, validation, and export behavior. Workspace manages folders, portable `.substrate` bundles, and federation intake. Studio, Mobile, and Hugo are projections over that same substrate rather than isolated silos pretending to be the knowledge itself.

That separation is the point. If the substrate outlives a projection, then the knowledge has not been taken hostage by a single product decision. A viewer can fail. A pricing model can change. A company can disappear. The substrate should still open somewhere else with its identities, relationships, provenance, fragments, and raw assets intact.

This is where the protocol's three recurring failure claims come together. Document boundaries collapse knowledge structure. Copy-and-paste reuse breaks lineage. Local-first storage without federation still leaves knowledge trapped inside tool-specific habits. XanaNode tries to solve all three by making the structure explicit and portable.

The Linux comparison also clarifies why federation matters. Linux succeeded because many independent actors could share a common base without surrendering ownership of their own layers. XanaNode aims for the same pattern with independently authored substrates that can be mounted, compared, merged, and projected without flattening disagreement or provenance.

The goal is not one more application. The goal is an open substrate ecosystem for durable human-to-machine knowledge transfer.

## Transcluded anchor passages

This essay transcludes four addressable fragments from the authored source node `xananode.canonical:source/xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft`.

- `xananode.canonical:source/xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft@sha256:xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft-source-v1#fragment/0001@sha256:xananode-kernel-thought-fragment-0001`
- `xananode.canonical:source/xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft@sha256:xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft-source-v1#fragment/0002@sha256:xananode-kernel-thought-fragment-0002`
- `xananode.canonical:source/xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft@sha256:xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft-source-v1#fragment/0003@sha256:xananode-kernel-thought-fragment-0003`
- `xananode.canonical:source/xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft@sha256:xananode-kernel-for-human-thought-draft-source-v1#fragment/0004@sha256:xananode-kernel-thought-fragment-0004`

Those tumbler addresses are the proof that the comparison can be preserved as referenceable structure instead of becoming another anonymous copy.

nodes.title: XanaNode compared to networked notes tools
nodes.summary: A comparison between XanaNode and note-linking tools such as Logseq, Obsidian, and Anytype, focusing on protocol-level portability, transclusion, lineage, and federation.
nodes.content:
Tools such as Logseq, Obsidian, and Anytype move closer to networked thought than ordinary flat documents do. They help people connect ideas, reuse material, and work locally with less dependence on one central service.

XanaNode is not a rejection of those tools. It is an attempt to define the missing protocol layer beneath them.

Logseq is strong at block-oriented thinking. Obsidian is strong at page-linking and a broad plugin ecosystem. Anytype is strong at object-oriented modeling and local-first ownership. What they do not yet provide in the same way is a shared, renderer-independent substrate contract for independently authored nodes, typed relationships, addressable fragments, portable raw assets, explicit provenance, mergeable federation, and many possible projection layers.

In other words, those tools can help a person think in a networked way. XanaNode tries to make the knowledge itself portable as a network, even when it leaves the original tool.

That is the deeper goal behind the comparison to Bush, Engelbart, Nelson, and Douglas Adams's Hyperland demonstration. The point is not just to create nicer linked notes. The point is to preserve the structure of thought, the source path behind it, and the assets that support it in a form that can survive transmission.

nodes.title: Relationships Preserve Knowledge Sentence
nodes.summary: Relationships preserve knowledge.

nodes.title: XanaNode Kernel Thought Fragment 0001
nodes.summary: The shared-plumbing comparison between Linux and XanaNode.
nodes.content: The Core SDK plays the role of shared plumbing. It is renderer-independent and handles the baseline rules of structure, relationship typing, fragment tracking, validation, and export so every projection does not have to reinvent them.

nodes.title: XanaNode Kernel Thought Fragment 0002
nodes.summary: The distributions-versus-projections comparison.
nodes.content: The substrate and the projection are intentionally separated. In Linux, people interact through distributions such as Ubuntu, Fedora, or Red Hat while the kernel remains the underlying contract. In XanaNode, the same substrate can be viewed through Studio, Mobile, Hugo, or a future projection that does not exist yet. The tool is a lens over the data, not the data itself.

nodes.title: XanaNode Kernel Thought Fragment 0003
nodes.summary: The tool-hostage and portability argument.
nodes.content: That separation matters because it prevents knowledge from being held hostage by one product. If a proprietary notes tool collapses, changes pricing, or drifts away from its users, the knowledge goes with it. XanaNode keeps the substrate local, protocol-shaped, and portable, so another compatible projection can pick it up and keep going.

nodes.title: XanaNode Kernel Thought Fragment 0004
nodes.summary: The federation and ecosystem argument.
nodes.content: Federation is the ecosystem layer. Linux did not become important because one company owned all computation. It mattered because many independent actors could build on a common base. XanaNode aims for the same pattern with independently authored substrates that can mount, compare, and merge without surrendering provenance, lineage, disagreement, or ownership.

nodes.title: Christian Siefen
nodes.summary: Author of XanaNode and maintainer of its canonical substrate, carrying the project from the Douglas Adams and Hyperland lineage toward a durable knowledge protocol.
nodes.content:
Christian Siefen is the human author of XanaNode and the maintainer of its canonical substrate.

This project did not begin as a detached software exercise. It comes out of the older human-augmentation lineage carried through Vannevar Bush, Douglas Engelbart, Ted Nelson, and the way Douglas Adams made that problem feel vivid and public through Hyperland and the broader Hitchhiker's worldview of interconnected meaning.

XanaNode is where that line of thought becomes an implemented protocol and working stack. The goal is not only to publish documents about knowledge, but to preserve the relationships, provenance, lineage, disagreement, fragments, and raw assets that make knowledge durable when it moves between tools and people.

XanaNode.com is the stable public home of that work. The GitHub profile at kingc95 is the public development surface where the protocol, Core SDK, Workspace, Studio, Mobile, Hugo, and related substrate repositories continue to evolve.

nodes.title: World Wide Web
nodes.summary: The public networked space where XanaNode domains, repositories, documentation, and published substrates are reached.

nodes.title: XanaNode Core SDK
nodes.summary: Renderer-independent parser, validator, graph builder, fragment engine, pack composer, suggestion engine, and exporter for XanaNode substrates.

nodes.title: XanaNode Hugo Theme
nodes.summary: Static-site renderer and graph viewer for XanaNode-compatible substrates.

nodes.title: XanaNode Mobile
nodes.summary: Android capture companion for quick substrate intake, field notes, media capture, file import, URL import, QR intake, and portable .substrate handoff.

nodes.title: XanaNode Protocol
nodes.summary: Canonical XanaNode protocol repository containing schemas, registries, specs, governance, examples, branding, and proposals.

nodes.title: XanaNode Studio
nodes.summary: Electron authoring workbench for creating nodes, editing protocol metadata, navigating graph context, and previewing projection layers.

nodes.title: XanaNode Workspace
nodes.summary: Local-first workspace engine for creating, opening, validating, building, importing, and snapshotting XanaNode substrates.

nodes.title: XNP-0004 Merge Validation
nodes.summary: A protocol proposal describing merge validation for independently authored substrates.

nodes.title: How do I use XanaNode myself?
nodes.summary: Start with a substrate, add nodes and typed relationships, validate it with Core, and project or capture it with tools such as Studio, Mobile, or Hugo.
nodes.content: A user can begin with a small question, claim, source, or trail. Core can initialize a substrate, validate nodes and relationships, generate review suggestions, and build bundles. Mobile can capture notes, files, URLs, and field media into a compliant working substrate. Studio can open a substrate as a local working copy so authors can edit without pretending they own someone else's substrate. Hugo can mount or import validated substrate data for a public website.

nodes.title: How do sharing rules work?
nodes.summary: Nodes are shareable by default, but a substrate can exclude individual nodes, relationships, trails, or selected groups from shared exports.
nodes.content: XanaNode assumes portability unless an author says otherwise. A private workspace can hold a large substrate while its pack export omits sensitive records. Sharing rules can live on the substrate manifest or on individual nodes, and official tools must respect those rules before publishing, exporting, mounting, or federating records.

nodes.title: Is XanaNode a real working stack?
nodes.summary: Yes. XanaNode has a protocol, Core SDK, Workspace layer, Studio authoring application, Mobile capture companion, and Hugo projection layer that work together.
nodes.content: XanaNode is not only a design idea. The current stack includes protocol schemas and governance rules, a Core SDK for validation and pack building, Workspace for local substrate handling, Studio for desktop authoring, Mobile for capture-first substrate intake, and Hugo for static public projection. The pieces are versioned so a reader can trace what each part does and why it exists.

nodes.title: Who made XanaNode?
nodes.summary: XanaNode is authored by Christian Siefen. Built By Bots remains part of the project's historical lineage, but XanaNode.com is the long-term public home of the work.
nodes.content: The canonical project substrate identifies Christian Siefen as the human author of XanaNode. It also preserves historical lineage records, including the temporary Built By Bots bridge period, the current XanaNode.com domain, the public repositories, branding, and support links. That provenance matters because people should be able to trace who authored a substrate, how the public identity evolved, and where project work now lives.

nodes.title: Why does XanaNode exist?
nodes.summary: XanaNode exists to preserve relationships, provenance, disagreement, lineage, and reusable fragments as durable knowledge structure.
nodes.content: A normal website can publish text. XanaNode publishes the relationships behind the text. It is meant for authored knowledge substrates where sources, claims, trails, versions, transclusions, conflicts, and projection layers remain inspectable instead of disappearing into a flat page.

nodes.title: Protocol Implements Relationship Preservation
nodes.summary: A first-class relationship node representing that the XanaNode protocol implements the claim that relationships preserve knowledge.

nodes.title: Claim
nodes.summary: A specific assertion that can be supported, disputed, revised, deprecated, or rejected.

nodes.title: Communication
nodes.summary: A first-class communication event or artifact whose topology, directionality, participants, and pattern affect how knowledge moved.

nodes.title: Community
nodes.summary: A group organized around an identity, platform, shared work, practice, interest, or tradition.

nodes.title: Concept
nodes.summary: An idea, pattern, theory, failure mode, or explanatory structure. A concept is built from claims.

nodes.title: Essay
nodes.summary: A human-authored argument that connects claims, sources, observations, and concepts.

nodes.title: Event
nodes.summary: Something that happened at a time and optionally at a place.

nodes.title: Fragment
nodes.summary: A stable, addressable portion of another node, source, media object, or publication used for deep linking, quotation, citation, transclusion, and revision-aware reference.

nodes.title: Hypothesis
nodes.summary: A provisional explanation, prediction, or model that can be tested, supported, falsified, revised, or abandoned.

nodes.title: Item
nodes.summary: A discrete physical thing, product, ingredient, component, tool, or other tangible object that can be counted, assembled, consumed, used, or stored.

nodes.title: Knowledge Gap
nodes.summary: A known absence, uncertainty, missing source, missing citation, lost document, unknown author, or unresolved area in a substrate.

nodes.title: Media
nodes.summary: An image, video, audio clip, screenshot, diagram, document, or other media item with provenance.

nodes.title: Observation
nodes.summary: A real-world case, symptom, example, incident, failure, or pattern being analyzed.

nodes.title: Organization
nodes.summary: A company, institution, agency, lab, school, collective, standards body, nonprofit, or group.

nodes.title: Person
nodes.summary: A human actor who created, influenced, witnessed, argued, documented, or participated in something.

nodes.title: Place
nodes.summary: A physical or virtual location where people, projects, organizations, or events exist or occurred.

nodes.title: Problem
nodes.summary: A difficulty, failure mode, design challenge, risk, or open need that organizes inquiry and work.

nodes.title: Project
nodes.summary: A built, proposed, fictional, abandoned, or active system with a purpose and lineage.

nodes.title: Publication
nodes.summary: A released intellectual or creative work such as a book, article, paper, film, episode, or talk.

nodes.title: Question
nodes.summary: An explicit inquiry, prompt, research question, or unresolved ask that motivates investigation and can be answered, partially answered, disputed, or left open.

nodes.title: Node Type Registry
nodes.summary: The canonical registry of current XanaNode node types, subtypes, visual treatment hints, and frontmatter recommendations.

nodes.title: Relationship
nodes.summary: A first-class edge treated as a node because the relationship itself has history, evidence, dispute, or explanation.

nodes.title: Response
nodes.summary: A reply, answer, rebuttal, clarification, or follow-up that responds to a question, communication, claim, or prior response.

nodes.title: Revision
nodes.summary: An immutable historical version of another node.

nodes.title: Schema
nodes.summary: A rule, ontology, validation document, or governance definition used by XanaNode itself.

nodes.title: Source
nodes.summary: A reference work, transcript, paper, book, talk, article, dataset, or primary evidence item.

nodes.title: Technology
nodes.summary: A tool, protocol, model, platform, architecture, method, or technical system.

nodes.title: Trail
nodes.summary: A curated route through the graph.

nodes.title: Birth date
nodes.summary: Calendar date on which a person, organization, project, or entity began.

nodes.title: Currency value
nodes.summary: A monetary amount with an ISO 4217 currency code.

nodes.title: External identifier
nodes.summary: A typed identifier from an external authority or registry.

nodes.title: Geographic coordinates
nodes.summary: WGS84 latitude and longitude coordinates for a place or geographically located entity.

nodes.title: SI measurement
nodes.summary: A quantified physical or conceptual measurement expressed in SI-compatible units.

nodes.title: Property Registry
nodes.summary: The canonical registry of standardized open-ended node properties for dates, coordinates, money, measurements, and external identifiers.

nodes.title: Property Registry Schema
nodes.summary: The JSON Schema for declaring standardized custom node properties such as measurements, coordinates, money values, identifiers, and dates.

nodes.title: Protocol Contexts
nodes.summary: The linked-data context files published by the XanaNode protocol.

nodes.title: Contexts / XanaNode Context
nodes.summary: The JSON-LD context that maps XanaNode terms into linked-data form.

nodes.title: Protocol Examples
nodes.summary: The bundled protocol examples used to teach substrate structure, federation, and review behavior.

nodes.title: Examples / Federation Example / Merge Report
nodes.summary: The merge report for the federation example.
nodes.content:
{
  "id": "example.merge:report/federation-example-001",
  "status": "review-needed",
  "generated_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z",
  "substrates": [
    "example.researcher_a",
    "example.researcher_b"
  ],
  "compatibility_level": 4,
  "mappings": [
    {
      "id": "example.merge:mapping/knowledge-memory-substrate",
      "source": "example.researcher_a:concept/knowledge-substrate",
      "target": "example.researcher_b:concept/memory-substrate",
      "type": "possible_match",
      "confidence": 0.78,
      "status": "review-needed",
      "preserves_local_nodes": true,
      "creates_relationships": [
        "example.researcher_a:rel/knowledge-substrate-possible-match-memory-substrate",
        "example.researcher_b:rel/memory-substrate-possible-match-knowledge-substrate"
      ],
      "rationale": "Both nodes describe substrate-like structures for preserving context and relationships, but one emphasizes knowledge objects while the other emphasizes memory over time."
    }
  ],
  "conflicts": [
    {
      "id": "example.merge:conflict/do-not-collapse-concepts",
      "type": "identity_ambiguity",
      "nodes": [
        "example.researcher_a:concept/knowledge-substrate",
        "example.researcher_b:concept/memory-substrate"
      ],
      "resolution": "preserve_both",
      "summary": "The merge pass records a possible match but does not replace either local concept with a canonical node."
    }
  ],
  "warnings": [
    "The two concepts are close but should not be automatically collapsed into a single canonical node."
  ]
}

nodes.title: Examples / Minimal Substrate / RO-Crate Metadata
nodes.summary: The RO-Crate companion metadata for the minimal substrate example.

nodes.title: Examples / Minimal Substrate / Substrate
nodes.summary: The substrate manifest for the minimal substrate example.
nodes.content:
{
  "id": "example.minimal",
  "name": "Minimal XanaNode Substrate",
  "description": "A tiny but complete XanaNode substrate showing that every meaningful node is connected through typed relationships, not only by a trail.",
  "version": "0.2.0",
  "namespace": "example.minimal",
  "repository": {
    "type": "git",
    "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/xananode.git",
    "default_branch": "main",
    "path": "examples/minimal-substrate"
  },
  "federation": {
    "feed": "https://example.com/xananode/minimal/feed.json",
    "merge_reports": "https://example.com/xananode/minimal/merge-reports/"
  },
  "trust": {
    "signed": false,
    "trust_scope": [
      "local_substrate",
      "assertion"
    ]
  },
  "schema_version": "xananode-core@0.5.0",
  "license": "CC0-1.0",
  "branding": {
    "brand_name": "XanaNode",
    "tagline": "Relationships preserve knowledge",
    "description": "Canonical XanaNode protocol branding used by protocol examples and downstream project tools.",
    "primary_media": "example.minimal:media/xananode-icon",
    "logo_media": "example.minimal:media/xananode-icon",
    "icon_media": "example.minimal:media/xananode-icon",
    "primary_color": "#55D6BE",
    "accent_color": "#FF8C00",
    "background_color": "#05070A",
    "text_color": "#F8FAFC",
    "source_node": "example.minimal:media/xananode-icon",
    "rights_status": "open-source",
    "license": "MIT",
    "updated_at": "2026-06-19T00:00:00Z",
    "reason": "Protocol branding is modeled as substrate metadata and media so changes to project identity can be preserved as knowledge."
  },
  "maintainers": [
    {
      "name": "Example Maintainer",
      "url": "https://example.com"
    }
  ],
  "imports": [
    "xananode:core"
  ]
}

      "description": "A substrate source built directly from the XanaNode Mobile repository, preserving the mobile application's identity, capture workflows, and key documents while carrying lower-level implementation files as attached assets unless they have independent semantic weight.",
      "status": "official",
      "repository": {
        "type": "git",
        "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Mobile.git",
        "default_branch": "main",
        "substrate_path": "substrate-source"
      },
      "recommended_mode": "mounted",
      "licenses": ["Apache-2.0"],
      "homepage": "https://xananode.com/"
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.hugo",
      "name": "XanaNode Hugo Theme Substrate",
      "namespace": "xananode.hugo",
      "description": "A substrate source built directly from the XanaNode Hugo repository, preserving the Hugo projection layer's identity, projection-facing documents, and key theme artifacts while carrying lower-level implementation files as attached assets unless they deserve their own nodes.",
      "status": "official",
      "repository": {
        "type": "git",
        "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Hugo.git",
        "default_branch": "main",
        "substrate_path": "substrate-source"
      },
      "recommended_mode": "mounted",
      "licenses": ["Apache-2.0"],
      "homepage": "https://xananode.com/"
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.alphabet",
      "name": "XanaNode Alphabet Substrate",
      "namespace": "xananode.alphabet",
      "description": "A deliberately small example substrate with a start trail, A-Z letter nodes, typed relationships, and bundled media assets for every letter.",
      "status": "example",
      "repository": {
        "type": "git",
        "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Alphabet-Substrate.git",
        "default_branch": "main"
      },
      "recommended_mode": "mounted",
      "licenses": ["CC0"],
      "homepage": "https://xananode.com/"
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.lineage",
      "name": "XanaNode Lost Lineage Substrate",
      "namespace": "xananode.lineage",
      "description": "A lineage substrate for the historical and conceptual path into XanaNode.",
      "status": "community",
      "repository": {
        "type": "git",
        "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Core-SDK.git",
        "default_branch": "main",

nodes.content:
{
  "implementations": [
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Protocol",
      "type": "protocol",
      "status": "active",
      "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode",
      "repository": "kingc95/XanaNode",
      "protocol_role": "schema-and-governance-source",
      "description": "Canonical XanaNode protocol repository containing schemas, registries, specs, governance, examples, branding, and proposals.",
      "consumes": [],
      "related_protocol_artifacts": [
        "../schemas/",
        "../registry/",
        "../specs/",
        "../governance/",
        "../examples/minimal-substrate/"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Core SDK",
      "type": "core-sdk",
      "status": "active",
      "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Core-SDK",
      "repository": "kingc95/XanaNode-Core-SDK",
      "protocol_role": "reference-core-engine",
      "description": "Renderer-independent parser, validator, graph builder, fragment engine, pack composer, suggestion engine, and exporter for XanaNode substrates.",
      "consumes": [
        "protocol schemas",
        "node files",
        "relationship files",
        "substrate manifests",
        "substrate packs"
      ],
      "related_protocol_artifacts": [
        "../schemas/substrate-manifest.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-node.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-relationships.schema.json",
        "../schemas/xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json",
        "../schemas/xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Hugo Theme",
      "type": "static-site-reference-implementation",
      "status": "active",
      "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/xananode-hugo",
      "repository": "kingc95/xananode-hugo",
      "protocol_role": "reference-renderer",
      "description": "Static-site renderer and graph viewer for XanaNode-compatible substrates.",
      "consumes": [
        "substrate manifests",
        "node files",
        "relationship files",
        "schema registries"
      ],
      "related_protocol_artifacts": [
        "../schemas/substrate-manifest.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-node.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-relationships.schema.json",
        "../examples/minimal-substrate/"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Workspace",
      "type": "workspace-engine",
      "status": "active",
      "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Workspace",
      "repository": "kingc95/XanaNode-Workspace",
      "protocol_role": "local-first-workspace-layer",
      "description": "Local-first workspace engine for creating, opening, validating, building, importing, and snapshotting XanaNode substrates.",
      "consumes": [
        "Core SDK APIs",
        "substrate manifests",
        "node files",
        "relationship files",
        "author profiles"
      ],
      "related_protocol_artifacts": [
        "../schemas/author-profile.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-manifest.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-node.schema.json"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Studio",
      "type": "desktop-authoring-application",
      "status": "active",
      "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Studio",
      "repository": "kingc95/XanaNode-Studio",
      "protocol_role": "desktop-authoring-interface",
      "description": "Electron authoring workbench for creating nodes, editing protocol metadata, navigating graph context, and previewing projection layers.",
      "consumes": [
        "Workspace APIs",
        "Core SDK APIs",
        "protocol registries",
        "protocol branding"
      ],
      "related_protocol_artifacts": [
        "../schemas/xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json",
        "../schemas/xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json",
        "../media/images/xananode-icon.svg"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Mobile",
      "type": "mobile-capture-companion",
      "status": "active",
      "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Mobile",
      "repository": "kingc95/XanaNode-Mobile",
      "protocol_role": "mobile-capture-interface",
      "description": "Android capture companion for quick substrate intake, field notes, media capture, file import, URL import, QR intake, and portable .substrate handoff.",
      "consumes": [
        "Workspace APIs",
        "Core SDK APIs",
        "protocol registries",
        "protocol branding",
        ".substrate bundles"
      ],
      "related_protocol_artifacts": [
        "../schemas/author-profile.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-manifest.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-node.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-relationships.schema.json",
        "../media/images/xananode-icon.svg"
      ]
    }
  ]
}

nodes.title: Schemas / XanaNode Substrate Layout v0.1.0
nodes.summary: A canonical schema or registry artifact published as XanaNode Substrate Layout v0.1.0.
nodes.content:
{
  "$schema": "./substrate-layout.schema.json",
  "id": "xananode.substrate-layout",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "description": "The standard directory layout for a portable XanaNode substrate root.",
  "root_contract": "A substrate root is the directory containing substrate.json. Tools should be able to validate, pack, mount, and project a substrate from this root without needing private application state.",
  "required_paths": [
    {
      "path": "substrate.json",
      "role": "substrate_manifest",
      "required": true,
      "kind": "file",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "The substrate manifest declaring id, name, namespace, version, repository, imports, sharing policy, and build/provenance metadata.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    }
  ],
  "recognized_paths": [
    {
      "path": "nodes/*.json",
      "role": "protocol_nodes",
      "kind": "glob",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Canonical protocol node records. A hand-built substrate may use this directory directly.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "nodes.json",
      "role": "protocol_node_bundle",
      "kind": "file",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "An alternate bundled protocol node artifact with a top-level nodes array.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "relationships.json",
      "role": "protocol_relationships",
      "kind": "file",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Typed relationship records for the substrate.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "assets/**",
      "role": "portable_media_and_source_assets",
      "kind": "glob",
      "description": "Images, PDFs, audio, video, source snapshots, projection media, documents, and other files carried by the substrate.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "schemas/*.json",
      "role": "local_schema_extensions",
      "kind": "glob",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Namespaced node type, relationship type, property, layout, or validation extensions.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "reports/*.json",
      "role": "reports",
      "kind": "glob",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Merge, compatibility, health, validation, duplicate-file, and intake reports.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "packs/*",
      "role": "mounted_substrate_packs",
      "kind": "glob",
      "description": "Unpacked substrate packs mounted into this substrate for local federation.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "imports/*",
      "role": "incoming_substrate_material",
      "kind": "glob",
      "description": "Dropped-in incoming packs or source material awaiting review, mounting, importing, or merge.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "author-profile.json",
      "role": "default_author_profile",
      "kind": "file",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Portable default author profile for hand-built or minimal substrates.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "ro-crate-metadata.json",
      "role": "research_object_metadata",
      "kind": "file",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Optional RO-Crate metadata describing the substrate as a research object.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    }
  ],
  "authoring_paths": [
    {
      "path": "content/nodes/*.md",
      "role": "markdown_authoring_nodes",
      "kind": "glob",
      "format": "markdown_frontmatter",
      "description": "Markdown authoring projection used by Core, Workspace, Studio, and Hugo workflows. Tools may generate protocol nodes from these files.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": ".xananode/**",
      "role": "workspace_metadata",
      "kind": "glob",
      "description": "Local workspace metadata such as preview settings, author lists, caches, and opened-source copies. Public exports may omit private caches but must preserve metadata needed for authored workspace round trips.",
      "tools_must_preserve": false
    }
  ],
  "archive_rules": [
    "A .substrate archive should contain every recognized path unless sharing policy excludes it.",
    "Tools must not include .git, node_modules, public renderer output, transient caches, or generated build directories unless explicitly requested.",
    "Every local media/source file referenced by asset_path, file, source_snapshot, primary media resolution, or registry projection metadata must be included or reported as missing.",
    "Duplicate files should be reported by content hash rather than silently duplicated without provenance.",
    "A hand-built substrate that follows this layout should open in Core, Workspace, Studio, Hugo, or any compatible projection without private tool state."
  ]
}

nodes.title: Examples / Federation Example / README
nodes.summary: The README for Examples / Federation Example.
nodes.content:
# Federation Example

This example shows how two independently authored substrates can be compared and partially merged without forcing a single source of truth.

- `substrate-a/` models "knowledge substrate" as a concept.
- `substrate-b/` models "memory substrate" as a similar concept.
- `review-workspace/` shows the temporary mounted review state where both concepts stay visible together with their possible-match overlap.
- each substrate records an outbound `possible_match` relationship to the other substrate's local concept.
- `merge-report.json` records the result of a federation/merge pass, including the decision to preserve both local concepts instead of collapsing them.
- `compatibility-report.json` records the practical compatibility level reached by the example.
- `substrate-diff.json` shows the exact node and relationship additions that appear when the two substrates are analyzed together.

The merge report does not overwrite either substrate. It records a reviewable mapping, keeps both local nodes intact, and makes the overlap explicit enough for tools to compare, mount, inspect, or later merge with human approval. The review workspace exists so the example does not stop at prose about merge behavior; it also shows an openable temporary substrate where the overlap is already visible.

nodes.title: Examples / Federation Example / Review Workspace / README
nodes.summary: The README for Examples / Federation Example / Review Workspace.
nodes.content:
# Federation Review Workspace

This example is the temporary review view a XanaNode tool can expose after mounting both federation example substrates together.

- It keeps `example.researcher_a:concept/knowledge-substrate` and `example.researcher_b:concept/memory-substrate` as separate nodes.
- It makes their `possible_match` overlap visible instead of silently collapsing them.
- It adds a short trail and review concept so the mounted result can be opened as a real substrate, not only read as reports.

This is not the only possible review projection. It is one valid example of the non-destructive merge state that sits between "independently authored substrates" and "human-approved permanent merge."

nodes.title: Examples / Minimal Substrate / Nodes / README
nodes.summary: The README for Examples / Minimal Substrate / Nodes.
nodes.content:
# Nodes

Each file in this directory is a node.

Every node in this example includes relationship references. This is intentional: the example should demonstrate a connected substrate, not just isolated nodes plus a trail.

The canonical relationship list lives in `../relationships.json`.

nodes.title: Examples / Minimal Substrate / README
nodes.summary: The README for Examples / Minimal Substrate.
nodes.content:
# Minimal XanaNode Substrate Example

This is the smallest useful XanaNode substrate that still follows the core rule:

> Nodes should not float as isolated facts. Concepts are built from claims, claims are supported by sources, and trails are curated routes through an already-connected graph.

This example uses only canonical XanaNode Core node types:

- `source`
- `concept`
- `claim`
- `person`
- `essay`
- `fragment`
- `trail`

It also uses canonical relationship types:

- `supports`
- `discusses`
- `related_to`
- `defines`
- `has_claim`
- `starts_with`
- `continues_to`
- `features`
- `created`
- `derived_from`
- `transcludes`

## What this example demonstrates

The graph is intentionally tiny, but it is not just a bag of nodes.

```text
As We May Think
  supports
    A knowledge substrate is structure, not just information
      defines
        Knowledge Substrate

As We May Think
  discusses
    Associative Trails
      related_to
        Knowledge Substrate

Start Here
  starts_with
    As We May Think

Relationship Navigation Intro
  transcludes
    As We May Think Fragment 0004
```

## Why the relationships matter

A normal hyperlink can show that the source, claim, and concept are somehow related.

XanaNode requires the relationship to say **how** they are related:

- The source `supports` the claim.
- The claim `defines` the concept.
- The concept `has_claim` so it is not unsupported.
- The source `discusses` associative trails.
- Associative trails are `related_to` the knowledge substrate as historical and conceptual context.
- The trail provides a human reading route, but it is not the only connection.
- The essay `transcludes` a fragment using the versioned tumbler address `example.minimal:source/as-we-may-think@git:8e47e70:examples/minimal-substrate/nodes/source-as-we-may-think.json#fragment/0004@sha256:example-as-we-may-think-fragment-0004`.

## Files

- `substrate.json` declares the substrate namespace and schema version.
- `relationships.json` declares canonical typed edges.
- `nodes/*.json` are individually addressable nodes with relationship references.

nodes.title: Examples / README
nodes.summary: The README for Examples.
nodes.content:
# XanaNode Examples

These examples are protocol examples, not full applications.

They show how a substrate can declare a namespace, define nodes, create typed relationships, and produce a federation merge report.

## Examples

- `minimal-substrate/` - the smallest useful XanaNode substrate.
- `federation-example/` - two independently authored substrates plus a merge report.

The examples intentionally use JSON files instead of a specific CMS, static-site generator, database, or graph renderer. Implementations may store the same structures in Markdown front matter, a database, RDF, graph stores, or other formats.

nodes.title: Governance / Canonical Type Policy
nodes.summary: A governance document covering Canonical Type Policy.
nodes.content:
# Canonical Type Policy

Canonical types are the shared vocabulary that make independent XanaNode substrates interoperable.

A type should become canonical only when it is:

- broadly useful across many domains
- clearly distinguishable from existing types
- describable in plain language
- stable enough to validate
- useful to both humans and machines

Avoid adding canonical types for every domain-specific object. Prefer subtypes or namespaced extensions.

## Projection Style Governance

Canonical relationship types include visual projection metadata. Every canonical relationship type must define:

- `color`
- `inverse_color`
- `line_style`
- `inverse_line_style`

These values are part of the shared vocabulary. Renderers use them to stratify connective lines so relationship meaning remains visible in graph projections, trails, maps, and other views.

Implementations may adapt the exact rendered appearance for accessibility, contrast, print, color-blind themes, or device constraints, but they should not collapse all relationship types into one undifferentiated line treatment while claiming full protocol projection compliance.

## Multi-Role Node Projection

The primary node `type` remains the authoritative validation and routing category. Secondary roles belong in `facets`, and narrower labels belong in `subtype` or `subtypes`.

Projection layers should preserve visible evidence of every recognized role. When a node has facets that match known node types, the projected mark should mix the primary type color with the facet type colors. Slices, rings, bands, layered borders, or another accessible mixed treatment are all valid. The important governance rule is that the projection must not silently flatten a multi-role node into one color when the substrate says the node participates in several modeling categories.

Schema vocabulary terms, schema fields, property registry entries, relationship categories, subtype entries, governance rules, and projection style rules should be modeled as `schema` nodes with specific subtypes. Those terms are part of the substrate vocabulary, not private implementation comments.

## Canonical vs Extension

Use canonical types for common substrate structure.

Use extensions for domain-specific modeling.

Example:

- `xananode:media` should be canonical.
- `museum:artifact` should be an extension.
- `biology:species` should be an extension unless future adoption proves otherwise.

nodes.title: Governance / Dispute Resolution
nodes.summary: A governance document covering Dispute Resolution.
nodes.content:
# Dispute Resolution

XanaNode treats disputes as knowledge, not merely failure.

A disputed claim should remain visible when appropriate, along with:

- who disputes it
- what evidence supports it
- what counterevidence challenges it
- what assumptions differ
- what sources are involved

Disputes can be represented with relationships such as:

- `contradicts`
- `contradicted_by`
- `disputes`
- `disputed_by`
- `alternative_to`
- `possible_match`
- `disputed_identity`

The goal is not instant consensus. The goal is navigable uncertainty.

nodes.title: Governance / Federation Rules
nodes.summary: A governance document covering Federation Rules.
nodes.content:
# Federation Rules

Federation connects independently authored substrates without forcing one source of truth.

A federation process should:

1. Load each substrate manifest.
2. Resolve each substrate's Git repository metadata.
3. Validate each substrate against declared schemas.
4. Preserve original node IDs and namespaces.
5. Detect likely overlaps.
6. Generate mapping relationships.
7. Preserve conflicts and uncertainty.
8. Avoid destructive merging.

Federation should create more knowledge, not erase local context.

## Never Collapse by Default

Two nodes that look similar should not automatically become one node.

Prefer:

- `same_entity`
- `possible_match`
- `disputed_identity`
- `equivalent_to`

depending on confidence and review status.

nodes.title: Governance / Moderation Model
nodes.summary: A governance document covering Moderation Model.
nodes.content:
# Moderation Model

XanaNode does not require one universal moderation authority.

Each substrate controls its own moderation rules.

Federation allows independently moderated substrates to be analyzed together while preserving their local decisions.

A substrate may choose to:

- approve nodes
- reject nodes
- deprecate claims
- preserve disputed claims
- hide low-confidence relationships
- publish merge reports

Moderation actions should themselves be traceable whenever possible.

nodes.title: Governance / README
nodes.summary: A governance document covering README.
nodes.content:
# XanaNode Governance

XanaNode governance defines how the protocol evolves while allowing independent substrates to remain sovereign.

The governance model is intentionally minimal:

1. Maintain a small stable core.
2. Allow namespaced extensions.
3. Preserve disagreement rather than deleting it.
4. Treat mappings, disputes, and merge results as knowledge.
5. Avoid centralizing truth in one moderation team.

Governance applies to the protocol repository. It does not dictate how every independent substrate must moderate its own content.

nodes.title: Governance / Schema Extension Rules
nodes.summary: A governance document covering Schema Extension Rules.
nodes.content:
# Schema Extension Rules

Substrates may define custom node and relationship types.

Custom types must be:

- namespaced
- versioned
- human-readable
- machine-readable
- preserved even when not understood
- optionally mapped to a core type

A valid extension should include:

- `id`
- `namespace`
- `type`
- `label`
- `purpose` or `meaning`
- `core: false`
- optional `extends`

Unknown extension types should not break import. They should be marked opaque and preserved.

nodes.title: Governance / Versioning Policy
nodes.summary: A governance document covering Versioning Policy.
nodes.content:
# Versioning Policy

XanaNode protocol files use semantic versioning where practical.

- Patch versions fix wording or validation mistakes.
- Minor versions add compatible types, fields, or examples.
- Major versions change compatibility expectations.

Schemas should be versioned independently from individual substrates.

Substrates should declare which core schema version they target.

Substrate history should be tracked in Git. Protocol version fields describe schema compatibility; Git commits describe concrete substrate revisions.

Example:

```json
{
  "schema_version": "xananode-core@0.5.0"
}
```

Breaking changes should go through the proposal process.

nodes.content:

`CC-BY-4.0`

You may share and adapt the documentation with attribution.

## Schemas, Validators, And Reference Code

JSON Schemas, schema registries, validators, tooling, examples intended as machine-readable fixtures, and reference implementation code are licensed under:

`Apache-2.0`

You may use, modify, distribute, and implement these artifacts under the Apache License 2.0.

## Trademarks

The XanaNode name, XanaNode logo, and related brand assets are trademarks or trade dress of the XanaNode project. License grants for documentation, schemas, validators, and code do not grant trademark rights.

See [TRADEMARK.md](TRADEMARK.md).

nodes.title: Proposals / README
nodes.summary: A XanaNode proposal covering README.
nodes.content:
# XanaNode Proposals

XanaNode Proposals, or XNPs, are design records for protocol changes.

Use proposals for changes that affect:

- canonical schemas
- federation behavior
- namespace rules
- compatibility levels
- transclusion
- tumbler addressing
- Git-backed substrates
- derived inverse relationships
- standards alignment profiles
- federation discovery and trust
- Studio local-first authoring
- governance

Proposal statuses:

- Draft
- Review
- Accepted
- Rejected
- Superseded
- Experimental

nodes.title: Proposals / XNP 0001 Namespaces
nodes.summary: A XanaNode proposal covering XNP 0001 Namespaces.
nodes.content:
# XNP-0001: Namespaces

Status: Accepted

## Summary

Defines how substrates and extension schemas avoid ID collisions.

## Motivation

XanaNode aims to support independently authored knowledge substrates that can later interoperate. This proposal records the design expectations for namespaces so implementations can converge without requiring centralized control.

## Requirements

- Must remain human-readable.
- Must remain machine-interpretable.
- Must preserve provenance.
- Must avoid destructive merging.
- Must allow independent substrate ownership.

## Open Questions

- What is the smallest useful implementation?
- Which parts are required for compatibility?
- Which parts should remain optional or experimental?

nodes.title: Proposals / XNP 0007 Git Backed Substrates
nodes.summary: A XanaNode proposal covering XNP 0007 Git Backed Substrates.
nodes.content:
# XNP-0007: Git-Backed Substrates

Status: Accepted

## Summary

Defines Git as the required versioning and history layer for production XanaNode substrates.

## Decision

Each production substrate is a Git repository. The substrate manifest must include a `repository` block describing the Git remote and default branch.

Git provides:

- revision history
- branching
- review
- merge
- synchronization
- rollback

XanaNode provides:

- node and relationship artifacts
- schema validation
- provenance fields
- merge and compatibility reports
- federation semantics

## Rationale

XanaNode should not reinvent distributed version control. Git already provides the substrate-level history model that knowledge authors need, while XanaNode adds knowledge-specific structure on top.

User-facing tools may rename Git concepts for approachability, such as "save snapshot" for commit or "draft path" for branch, but the underlying substrate history remains Git.

nodes.title: Proposals / XNP 0008 Derived Inverse Relationships
nodes.summary: A XanaNode proposal covering XNP 0008 Derived Inverse Relationships.
nodes.content:
# XNP-0008: Derived Inverse Relationships

Status: Accepted

## Summary

Defines inverse relationship labels as derived views rather than separate canonical relationship types.

## Decision

Canonical relationship types are authored in one direction only. A relationship type may declare an `inverse` label for display, querying, or generated indexes, but the inverse should not be registered as a second canonical type.

Example:

```json
{
  "type": "created",
  "inverse": "created_by"
}
```

A relationship record should store:

```json
{
  "source": "example:person/alice",
  "target": "example:essay/intro",
  "type": "created"
}
```

An implementation may display `created_by` when viewing the target node, but should not store a second `created_by` relationship type or edge.

## Rationale

Registering both directions bloats the canonical vocabulary and creates avoidable consistency risks:

- duplicate relationship type definitions
- mismatched inverse pairs
- dangling inverse types
- unnecessary authoring choices
- harder validation

Derived inverse views keep the graph navigable in both directions without doubling the protocol vocabulary.

nodes.title: Proposals / XNP 0009 Standards Alignment
nodes.summary: A XanaNode proposal covering XNP 0009 Standards Alignment.
nodes.content:
# XNP-0009: Standards Alignment Profiles

Status: Accepted

## Summary

Defines optional interoperability profiles for JSON-LD, Web Annotation selectors, PROV-O, content IDs, RO-Crate, and nanopublication-style claim packages.

## Decision

XanaNode keeps its native artifacts simple, but provides mappings and companion profiles for standards-aware tools.

Accepted profiles:

- JSON-LD context
- Web Annotation selector names
- PROV-O provenance mapping
- optional content IDs and version IDs
- RO-Crate-compatible preservation metadata
- nanopublication-like claim packaging

These profiles are optional for basic substrates but recommended for archival, scholarly, legal, scientific, and federated deployments.

nodes.title: Proposals / XNP 0010 Federation Discovery And Trust
nodes.summary: A XanaNode proposal covering XNP 0010 Federation Discovery And Trust.
nodes.content:
# XNP-0010: Federation Discovery and Trust

Status: Accepted

## Summary

Defines optional discovery and trust metadata in substrate manifests.

## Decision

Substrate manifests may include:

- federation inbox
- federation outbox
- subscription or feed URL
- merge report endpoint
- mapping endpoint
- signature metadata
- public key metadata
- trust scopes

Trust scopes are:

- `local_substrate`
- `imported_schema`
- `mapping`
- `assertion`

## Rationale

Git-backed substrates can federate through repository exchange, but active federation benefits from discoverable update and mapping endpoints. Signatures and public keys provide a path toward verifiable manifests, merge reports, mappings, and assertions.

nodes.title: Proposals / XNP 0011 Studio Local First Authoring
nodes.summary: A XanaNode proposal covering XNP 0011 Studio Local First Authoring.
nodes.content:
# XNP-0011: Studio Local-First Authoring

Status: Draft

## Summary

Captures the Studio product direction as local-first Git-backed authoring for knowledge substrates.

## Decision

Studio should treat a substrate as a local Git repository and expose user-facing concepts before raw Git mechanics.

Author-facing language:

- save snapshot
- draft path
- bring changes together
- propose changes
- what changed
- history

The Studio MVP should focus on opening substrate repos, editing nodes, adding relationships, validating, previewing with the Hugo implementation, saving snapshots, and publishing.

nodes.title: Proposals / XNP 0012 Executable Tool Contract
nodes.summary: A XanaNode proposal covering XNP 0012 Executable Tool Contract.
nodes.content:
# XNP-0012: Executable Tool Contract

## Status

Draft

## Summary

Define a stable executable contract for official XanaNode tooling so applications can call Core and Workspace behavior without embedding Node.js directly.

## Motivation

XanaNode is a protocol, not a JavaScript framework.

Right now, the reference stack is implemented in Node-based repositories:

- XanaNode-Protocol
- XanaNode-Core-SDK
- XanaNode-Workspace
- XanaNode-Hugo
- XanaNode-Studio
- XanaNode-Mobile

That is acceptable as an implementation phase, but it is not the right long-term dependency story for the protocol itself.

If a desktop app, mobile app, static-site builder, AI agent, or institutional integration must install Node and call private JavaScript modules directly, then the protocol boundary is too soft. The protocol should be callable from any stack that can execute a program, pass files, and read JSON results.

## Proposal

Official tooling should converge on two primary executable surfaces:

- `xananode-core`
- `xananode-workspace`

These names describe roles, not implementation language. They may be delivered as:

- native binaries
- packaged runtime executables
- OS-specific launchers
- language wrappers that preserve the same command contract

## Goals

1. Keep protocol behavior callable from any language or host environment.
2. Separate implementation language from protocol contract.
3. Give Studio, Mobile, Hugo helpers, AI agents, and third-party tools one stable thing to call.
4. Keep artifact generation, intake analysis, and projection derivation aligned across the stack.

## Non-goals

1. This proposal does not replace language SDKs.
2. This proposal does not forbid direct in-process library use.
3. This proposal does not require one operating-system packaging strategy.

## Core responsibilities

`xananode-core` should own:

- substrate validation
- protocol inspection
- artifact builds
- portable bundle generation
- fragment derivation
- review suggestion generation
- intake analysis
- projection-data derivation

Recommended commands:

- `validate`
- `inspect`
- `build`
- `bundle`
- `analyze-intake`
- `projection`

## Workspace responsibilities

`xananode-workspace` should own:

- substrate initialization
- workspace open/create behavior
- author and repository enforcement
- local build orchestration
- portable export
- mounted substrate intake
- working-copy creation
- de-intertwingle cleanup
- health reporting
- snapshot orchestration

Recommended commands:

- `init`
- `open`
- `build`
- `export`
- `intertwingle`
- `deintertwingle`
- `health`
- `snapshot`

## Input and output rules

Executable implementations should:

- accept filesystem paths to substrate folders, `.substrate` archives, `substrate-bundle.json`, and `substrate-bundle.jsonl`
- support structured JSON output for machine callers
- emit non-zero exit codes on failure
- expose build metadata including implementation version and commit when possible
- preserve protocol semantics regardless of implementation language

Machine callers should not be expected to scrape prose logs to determine success.

## Why this matters

XanaNode is supposed to let knowledge move across tools and media without losing structure.

The same principle applies to the tooling itself.

If the protocol is real, then:

- a Kotlin Android app should be able to call it
- a Rust CLI should be able to call it
- a Python workflow should be able to call it
- a static-site renderer should be able to call it
- an AI agent should be able to call it

without pretending JavaScript is the protocol.

## Transition path

1. Keep the current Node reference implementations.
2. Tighten their command contracts and JSON outputs.
3. Package them as self-contained executables for supported platforms.
4. Teach official tools to prefer the executable boundary.
5. Allow alternate implementations later as long as they preserve the same artifact rules and command semantics.

## Compatibility

This proposal is additive.

Existing repositories may continue to expose:

- Node modules
- npm CLIs
- internal helper APIs

But official documentation should increasingly describe the executable contract as the stable cross-stack surface.

nodes.title: XanaNode Protocol README
nodes.summary: The main human-readable overview of the XanaNode protocol.
nodes.content:
# XanaNode

![XanaNode Logo](media/images/xananode-icon.svg)

## What is XanaNode?

Short:

Relationships preserve knowledge.

Canonical:

XanaNode is a protocol for independently authored knowledge substrates that preserve relationships, provenance, lineage, disagreement, and addressable fragments, so knowledge can move across tools and media without losing its structure.

Expanded:

XanaNode is a protocol for independently authoring, transmitting, and federating knowledge substrates that preserve relationships, provenance, lineage, disagreement, and addressable fragments, enabling durable human-to-machine knowledge transfer through many possible projection layers.

Rather than treating documents as the primary unit of knowledge, XanaNode treats relationships as first-class entities and models knowledge as a connected network of people, concepts, claims, questions, hypotheses, problems, knowledge gaps, communications, responses, sources, events, media, organizations, technologies, and their relationships.

XanaNode is designed to be both human-readable and machine-interpretable.

XanaNode is also a working software stack.
